I'm Eniola Aromolaran — a designer who leads with structure first. Three years designing brand identity and campaigns for two organisations, plus a self-taught UI practice in Figma covering flows, checkout, and onboarding.

View Work → Get in touch

About

01 / introduction

I design interfaces and brand visuals that feel considered — clean layouts, clear hierarchy, and a point of view. For the past three years I've led visual design for two organisations: a mental health advocacy initiative and a climate-focused nonprofit, building each one's visual identity from scratch and keeping it consistent across everything they put out.

Alongside that, I've been teaching myself UI/UX design in Figma — working through real interface problems like checkout flows, onboarding, and account settings, the same way someone might work through scales before a recital.

I also spent time in IT support, which sounds unrelated but isn't really — it's where I got comfortable with deadlines, structure, and not cutting corners. I bring that same care into design: I'd rather get the spacing right than ship something "close enough." I'm looking to bring that into a full-time role where I can grow from solid fundamentals into senior-level UI and brand work.

Selected Work

02 / seven case studies
FRAME / 00 — LEAD PROJECT · REAL CLIENT

Folta Express Cafe

Real ClientWeb DesignResponsiveDesign System3 Breakpoints

The brief: Folta Express Cafe — a dessert cafe in Ibadan — needed a website that matched the energy of walking into the space: warm, indulgent, and inviting. It also needed to work as a real business tool: drive online orders, show off the menu, and handle event bookings.

I took the project from zero — no brand guidelines, no existing web presence — and delivered a full website design across desktop, tablet, and mobile, plus a style guide for the team to build on.

What I focused on

  • Hero section uses an atmospheric cafe photo full-bleed — you feel the space before you read a word. The headline leads with personality ("Life's Sweeter") and immediately answers the two questions every visitor has: what do you sell, and can I order now?
  • Two primary CTAs from the very first screen — "Order Online" and "Visit Our Cafe" — because the business has two different conversion goals and both matter.
  • Social proof bar (100+ items, 4.8 rating, 5000+ customers) placed at the base of the hero, not buried in an about page — people trust numbers they see early.
  • Responsive across all three breakpoints — desktop, tablet, and mobile — with the layout genuinely restructured at each size, not just shrunk.
  • Design system included — colour palette, typography scale (DM Serif Display for display, Poppins for body), icon style, button variants, and card components, so whoever builds this has everything they need.

Where I'd take it further

I'd want to add a live menu filter by category and explore an online ordering flow end-to-end — right now the CTA links out, but keeping the user on-site through checkout would reduce drop-off significantly.

FRAME / 01 — UI & LAYOUT

Syntax Real Estate

UI DesignLayoutWeb

Real estate sites usually drown buyers in information before they've even seen a house. I designed a landing page that leads with the emotional hook — "find your perfect home" — and only then earns its way into listings, pricing, and trust.

What I focused on

  • A simple hero: bold headline, one supporting line, one action. No competing for attention.
  • A scrollable "Popular Properties" row so price and type are visible at a glance.
  • A trust-building CTA block placed after the listings, not before — once someone's browsed, that's the moment to pitch credibility.
  • A quieter blog section at the bottom that supports SEO without competing with the main goal: get people searching.

Where I'd take it further

Testing property card density at 3 vs. 4 visible, and a sticky search bar so the primary action stays in reach while scrolling.

FRAME / 02 — UI & MOBILE

Daily UI — Sign In / Sign Up

UI DesignMobileAuth Flow

The Daily UI brief was just "design this screen." I treated it as a chance to design a complete pair, because in real products, sign-in and sign-up never exist alone.

What I focused on

  • Identical structure across both screens — same spacing, button style, login order — so the pair reads as one continuous flow.
  • A single accent colour used only on the primary action, so there's never a question of what to tap.
  • Social logins placed below the form, since most returning users sign in with email first.

Where I'd take it further

Inline validation before submit, and reconsidering "Forgot password" placement — it's easy to miss next to the checkbox right now.

FRAME / 03 — E-COMMERCE

Checkout Flow

UI DesignE-commerceMobile

Checkout is one of the highest-stakes screens in any product — it's where carts get abandoned. The job here was to remove every bit of friction between "almost done" and "done."

What I focused on

  • Order summary stays visible above the form so the total is never a mystery.
  • Tabs across the top (Order details / Delivery / Payment) signal this is step 3 of a flow, not a one-off form.
  • A moody product photo keeps the brand premium without hurting form legibility, thanks to a dark overlay and high-contrast fields.

Where I'd take it further

A visible security cue near the card fields — a lock icon or "secure checkout" line. Trust signals matter more here than almost anywhere else in a flow.

FRAME / 04 — BRAND & CAMPAIGN

Sunshine × Oxfam — Care Is Work Campaign

Brand DesignCampaignReal ClientOxfam Supported

The brief: Sunshine Mental Health Initiative partnered with Oxfam on the "Unseen Burdens" project — a campaign documenting the invisible mental health toll of unpaid care work on women in Lagos. The campaign needed materials that were bold enough to stop someone mid-scroll, but sensitive enough to treat the subject with the weight it deserves.

This flyer was the anchor piece — used at outreach events, distributed in communities, and shared across social platforms.

What I focused on

  • The headline is the campaign in three words: "Care is Work. Full Stop." — no softening, no qualification. Setting that typographic hierarchy (black / red / black) made "WORK." the word people read first and remember longest.
  • The head silhouette filled with a photo grid does the conceptual work quietly — all those images of women cooking, cleaning, and caring are literally inside her head. You understand the idea before you read a sentence.
  • Structural split between left (emotional, human, copy-led) and right (data, visual concept) gives the eye two places to land — so the poster works whether someone reads it in full or glances for two seconds.
  • Yellow kept as the brand warmth even on a heavy subject — clinical grays would have killed the approachability the campaign needed for community outreach.

Outcome

The flyer was used across Oxfam-supported outreach events in Lagos and distributed through the Sunshine WhatsApp community. The campaign helped the organisation secure continued partnership with Oxfam for follow-up programming.

FRAME / 05 — PROBLEM SOLVING
Stage 1 — Wireframe
Stage 2 — Final UI

Sunshine — Volunteer Sign-Up Flow

WireframingUX FlowReal Client Problem

The problem: Sunshine had a volunteer interest form buried three taps deep on their Instagram bio link, with no way to tell people what they'd actually be signing up for. Interest was there — people commented on posts wanting to help — but it wasn't converting into sign-ups.

My approach: Before touching any screen, I mapped the flow a volunteer would need: see what we do → understand the roles → give minimal info → get a clear next step. Wireframing first let me test that structure without getting distracted by colour or imagery.

Decisions in the wireframe

  • Landing screen leads with what volunteering involves, not a form — people commit faster once they know what they're signing up for.
  • Sign-up screen asks for three fields only, plus one choice: which area they want to help with. Fewer fields, faster completion.
  • Confirmation screen gives an immediate next step ("Add to Calendar") instead of just a thank-you message, so interest doesn't go cold before the next event.

Outcome & next step

This wireframe is currently being built out into final UI and handed to the Sunshine team to replace the bio-link form. Next: usability-test the role-selection step with 3–4 real volunteers before finalising visuals.

FRAME / 06 — PROBLEM SOLVING
Stage 1 — Wireframe
Stage 2 — Final UI

Ecodiversified — Event & Donation Flow

WireframingUX FlowReal Client Problem

The problem: Ecodiversified runs clean-up events and relies on small donations to fund them, but the two asks were tangled together — people interested in attending an event had no clean way to just give money instead, and vice versa.

My approach: I split the two intents into a single flow with a fork, instead of two separate competing CTAs. Browsing events is the entry point either way; donating becomes one tap from any event, not a separate hunt.

Decisions in the wireframe

  • Events screen uses a simple grid so someone can scan dates and locations in seconds, with one clear way to see more.
  • Event detail puts location and date as scannable tags up top, and offers RSVP as the primary action — donation lives one tap away, not competing for the same button.
  • Support screen uses preset amounts (with one highlighted as a suggested default) plus a custom field, so giving takes one tap instead of typing for most people.

Outcome & next step

This wireframe is going to the Ecodiversified team for feedback before I move into visual design. Next: confirm which donation amount should be the suggested default based on past giving data.
